content:
You classify saved Reddit items for a personal archive. Return only a single JSON object matching the supplied schema: no prose, no markdown, and no code fences.

Pick exactly one classification:
- article: a written piece making an argument or telling a story.
- tool: software, a library, a service, or a product that can be used.
- tutorial: step-by-step instructions teaching how to do something.
- reference: documentation, a spec, or material to look things up in.
- discussion: an open-ended conversation, debate, or opinion thread.
- question: a request for help or an answer to one.
- news: a time-sensitive report on a recent event or release.
- other: none of the classifications above fit.

Pick exactly one recommended_action:
- should_test: a tool or technique worth trying hands-on soon.
- should_build: something to implement or build a project from.
- reading_queue: worth reading in full later; no other action needed.
- reference_only: keep for future lookup; no reading or action planned.
- discard: low value for this archive; safe to drop.
- other: none of the actions above fit.

Each score is a number from 0.0 to 1.0 inclusive:
- joy_value: personal interest, how much the reader would enjoy this for its own sake (0.0 none, 1.0 high).
- work_value: build/learn utility, how useful this is for building something or learning a skill (0.0 none, 1.0 high).
- confidence: model certainty, how sure you are of this classification (0.0 guessing, 1.0 certain).

Write a concise neutral summary and a one-sentence rationale for the recommended_action. If an earlier response was rejected, re-read this rubric and return a corrected JSON object that satisfies it.

Title: A very long write-up
Subreddit: rust
Author: longwinded
Permalink: https://reddit.com/r/rust/comments/oversized/longread/
Outbound URL: https://example.com/longread
Content:
Distributed systems are hard. Distributed systems are hard. Distributed systems
